journal-title-group.journal-title: The Journal of Veterinary Medical Science

title-group.article-title: A retrospective study of the correlation of intraocular pressure and ultrasound biomicroscopy parameters with cataract maturity in small breed dogs

contrib-group.aff: 1)Department of Veterinary Clinical Sciences, College of Veterinary Medicine and Research Institute for Veterinary Science, Seoul National University, Seoul, Republic of Korea

author-notes.corresp: *Correspondence to: Seo K: kmseo@snu.ac.kr, Department of Veterinary Clinical Sciences, College of Veterinary Medicine and Research Institute for Veterinary Science, Seoul National University, 1 Gwanak-ro, Gwanak-gu, Seoul 08826, Republic of Korea

permissions.copyright-statement: ©2025 The Japanese Society of Veterinary Science

license.license-p: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial No Derivatives (by-nc-nd) License. (CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

abstract.p: There is a clinical challenge in decision-making regarding prophylactic glaucoma treatment based solely on intraocular pressure (IOP) trends for long-term cataract management or before phacoemulsification surgery in canines. This study aimed to compare IOP and ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM) parameters with cataract maturity in small dog breeds. We conducted a retrospective review of medical records pertaining to ophthalmic examinations and UBM assessments of small breed dogs diagnosed with cataracts at the Veterinary Medical Teaching Hospital of Seoul National University from January 2020 to August 2024. UBM images obtained under topical anesthesia were analyzed to quantify various parameters. Subsequently, the 47 eyes examined were classified into four groups according to cataract maturity. The ciliary cleft area (CCA), ciliary cleft length (CCL) and width (CCW), and iridocorneal angle (ICA) were measured. The measured values were compared to determine their degree of correlation with cataract maturity. Statistical analyses of patient demographics were also performed. Both CCA (P=0.0024) and CCL (P=0.0004) decreased significantly with increasing cataract maturity. However, IOP, CCW, and ICA were not significantly associated with cataract maturity. Age and sex did not have significant effects on any dependent variable. Among the UBM parameters, CCA and CCL demonstrated a significant correlation with cataract maturity, indicating structural alterations in the ICA might develop in association with cataracts, independent of direct IOP effects. These findings underscore the importance of considering prophylactic glaucoma management in patients with advanced cataracts to mitigate potential complications.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Cataracts are a collective term for various lens-related disorders characterized by loss of transparency in the lens nucleus, cortex, or capsule. This condition may arise from genetic factors or other systemic or ocular health issues [18]. Lens-induced uveitis stemming from cataracts may influence intraocular pressure (IOP). Furthermore, glaucoma is a prevalent postoperative complication after phacoemulsification, the primary surgical procedure for the treatment of canine cataracts [3, 13, 14, 24, 25].

In dogs, disruption of the blood-aqueous barrier has been reported to occur in all cataract-affected eyes regardless of maturity level [6, 7, 22]. The lower IOP in eyes with mature to hypermature cataracts indicated a possible relationship between cataract severity and reduced IOP due to inflammatory processes [17]. However, the relationship between IOP and cataract development remains unclear.

Secondary glaucoma can result from iridocorneal angle (ICA) blockage due to uveitis, lens displacement, neoplastic cells, or other obstructions, including preiridal fibrovascular membranes [18, 20, 26]. Phacolytic open-angle glaucoma in dogs may also arise from lens-induced uveitis caused by the resorption of hypermature cataracts [18]. In humans, when phacolytic glaucoma progresses, it presents with classic symptoms such as clouding of the anterior chamber, corneal opacity, and increased IOP [4, 15].

Ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM) has been instrumental in identify structural changes associated with cataract progression. In a human case, several key features of phacolytic glaucoma were identified, highlighting the diagnostic potential of UBM in identifying structural changes associated with cataract progression and complications [15]. In canines, UBM studies have documented parameter shifts following mydriasis and phacoemulsification [5, 9]. Kim et al. also demonstrated increases in ciliary cleft area, width, and length in dogs with immature and mature cataracts compared to those with normal eyes or incipient cataracts [13]. Although cataracts are known to increase the glaucoma risk, no study to date has directly investigated structural changes in the iridocorneal angle across four stages of cataracts in dogs or their relationship to IOP. This study aimed to analyze how IOP and UBM parameters change across different cataract maturities to guide glaucoma drug prescription decisions.

sec.title: Selection of the animals
sec.p: Forty-seven dogs were selected from a retrospective review of medical records from the Seoul National University Veterinary Medical Teaching Hospital Ophthalmology Service between January 2020 and August 2024. These dogs were diagnosed with cataracts without other intraocular diseases, as confirmed by a thorough ophthalmological examination. Eyes with a history of clinically significant inflammation or diseases that could induce such inflammation were excluded from the study. Furthermore, any signs of previous inflammation, such as synechia or pigmentation, were routinely reviewed in electronic medical records, and no such records were found for the eyes included in this study. The examinations were carried out in the same room with consistent ambient light intensity and no injectable sedatives or topical ophthalmic drugs were used. It included basic neuro-ophthalmic evaluations, Schirmer Tear Test-1 (STT-1), IOP measurement using the rebound tonometry device (Tonovet®, iCare, Vantaa, Finland), fluorescein staining, slit-lamp biomicroscopy, and indirect ophthalmoscopy (Pan Retinal® 2.2, VOLK, Mentor, OH, USA). Examinations were carried out by trained ophthalmologists under the supervision of two veterinary ophthalmologists (S Kang and K Seo). This study was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Seoul National University (SNU-240603-8). The subjects were dogs of any breed, aged 0–16 years, of any sex, and without a history of underlying systemic conditions or intraocular surgery. Cataract maturity was classified as incipient, immature, mature, or hypermature. Of the 47 eyes included in this study, body weight information was available for 38 eyes. The mean, median, and range of body weight were 5.27 kg, 4.5 kg, and 1.88–11.7 kg, respectively. For the 9 eyes without body weight information, the breeds included Maltese (n=4), Toy/Miniature Poodles (n=3), Bichon Frise (n=1), and Boston Terrier (n=1). This study focused on small-breed dogs, as body size (and eyeball size) may influence UBM parameters.

sec.title: UBM measurements
sec.p: UBM examinations were performed after a thorough ophthalmic assessment. The instrument used for this study consisted of a 50 MHz probe (MD-320W®, MEDA, Tianjin, China). Before the examination, the canine subject was administered 0.5% proparacaine (Alcaine®, Alcon Laboratories Inc., Fort Worth, TX, USA) and placed in the sternal recumbency with gentle manual restraint, abstaining from sedation. The procedures were carried out in a standardized environment with consistent and uniformly dim yellow lighting. The aqueous outflow pathways in the dorsal quadrant were assessed. The transducer was placed perpendicular to the corneoscleral limbus at the 9–12 o’clock position. Serial images were captured from each live video sequence and selected for analysis by another examiner (Jang). The image was chosen to effectively show the drainage structures encompassing the corneoscleral limbus, iris root, ICA, and ciliary cleft (CC).

The following four parameters were measured for each eye: (a) the geometric ICA defined by the iris root and the posterior corneoscleral limbus, (b) the width of the entrance to the CC as the distance from the corneoscleral limbus to the iris root (CCW: ciliary cleft width), (c) the length of the CC measured from the pectinate ligament (or the most anterior visible part of the uveal trabecular meshwork) to the front edge of the ciliary body (CCL: ciliary cleft length), and (d) the cross-sectional area of the CC measured as hypoechoic to anechoic area outlined by CCW, CCL, and anterior surface of the iris root (CCA: ciliary cleft area) (Fig. 1Fig. 1.
Illustrative representation of the ciliary cleft parameterization using ultrasound biomicroscopy. Arrowhead indicates the iris root. Arrow marks the margin of Schwalbe’s line. Solid line illustrates the iridocorneal angle (ICA), established by a line drawn tangential to the sclera from the iris root (arrowhead) and extending to the margin of Schwalbe’s line (arrow), forming the angle. Dashed line represents the perpendicular distance from the iris root (arrowhead) to the extension of the ICA, intersecting the sclera to define the ciliary cleft width (CCW). Dotted line highlights the hyperechoic area within the ciliary cleft, indicating the ciliary cleft area (CCA), bounded by the inner and outer edges of the cleft. Bi-arrow line represents the length of the ciliary cleft (CCL), measured from its beginning near the hyperechoic area to its deepest point.). All measurements were performed using separate software (ImageJ®, National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, MD, USA).

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: All statistical analyses were performed with SPSS Statistics 26 software (IBM SPSS STATISTICS®, IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). A one-way analysis of variance was used to compare age, IOP, and UBM parameters across the four stages of cataract. The eyes were grouped according to the patient’s age at the time of examination. Patients aged ≤8 years were classified as “young patients (YP),” and those aged >9 years were classified as “senior patients (SP).” The same dataset for the YP and SP groups was analyzed using a two-sample t-test. The same statistical model was used for sex difference analysis.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: The mean ± standard deviation (SD) age of the 47 dogs included in this study was 8.9 ± 3.2, ranging from 0 to 16 years old. Eight different breeds were represented, with poodles (n=25), Bichon Frises (n=9), and Shih Tzu (n=6) being the most common. The remaining dogs included two Boston Terriers, two Maltese, and one each of the Cocker Spaniel, French Bulldog, and Maltipoo. The study sample consisted of 5 intact females, 19 spayed females, 2 intact males, and 21 neutered males. The number of cases diagnosed with incipient cataract, immature cataract, mature cataract, and hypermature cataract were 15, 13, 9, and 10, respectively.

The measurements of the dogs at each stage of cataract are shown in Fig. 2Fig. 2.
Representative images of CCL (yellow) and CCA (red) measurements from different cataract stages on ultrasound biomicroscopy. (A) Patient with incipient cataract, (B) patient with immature cataract, (C) patient with mature cataract, (D) patient with hypermature cataract.. A linear correlation was found between the progression of the cataract stage and CCA (P=0.024), showing that CCA decreased by approximately 0.073 mm2 for each 1 unit increase in cataract maturity. Furthermore, the CCL was significantly shortened with increasing cataract stage, decreasing by approximately 0.295 mm for each 1 unit increase in cataract maturity (P=0.004). The mean ± SD value of CCA was 0.613 mm2 ± 0.247 mm2, 0.467 mm2 ± 0.277 mm2, 0.459 mm2 ± 0.277 mm2, and 0.382 mm2 ± 0.283 mm2 in the incipient, immature, mature, and hypermature groups, respectively. Similarly, the mean ± SD value of CCL was 2.596 mm ± 0.962 mm, 2.172 mm ± 0.437 mm, 2.124 mm ± 0.825 mm, and 1.637 ± 0.697 in the incipient, immature, mature, and hypermature groups, respectively (Fig. 2, Table 1Table 1.Values of intraocular pressure and anterior segment parameters by cataract maturity stage
Cataract stageIOP (mmHg)ICA (°)CCW (mm)CCL (mm)CCA (mm2)
Incipient (n=15)13.4 ± 3.014.768 ± 3.3410.351 ± 0.0992.596 ± 0.962***0.613 ± 0.247**
Immature (n=13)13.3 ± 4.113.225 ± 4.7360.293 ± 0.1212.172 ± 0.437***0.467 ± 0.277**
Mature (n=9)12.2 ± 3.214.387 ± 4.4330.315 ± 0.1662.124 ± 0.825***0.459 ± 0.277**
Hypermature (n=10)13.1 ± 4.016.470 ± 5.2590.352 ± 0.1171.637 ± 0.697***0.382 ± 0.283**
Statistical significance was observed in the linear regression model evaluating the correlation between cataract stage and ciliary cleft parameters (***P=0.004, **P=0.024). Mean ± SD values are shown. IOP, intraocular pressure; ICA, iridocorneal angle; CCW, width of the ciliary cleft; CCL, length of the ciliary cleft. CCA, area of the ciliary cleft.).

The following results were observed for parameters that did not show statistically significant changes at different stages of cataract. The mean ± SD value of IOP was 13.4 mmHg ± 3.0 mmHg, 13.3 mmHg ± 4.1 mmHg, 12.2 mmHg ± 3.2 mmHg, and 13.1 mmHg ± 4.0 mmHg in the incipient, immature, mature, and hypermature groups, respectively (Table 1).

Similarly, the mean ± SD value of ICA was 14.768° ± 3.341°, 13.225° ± 4.736°, 14.387° ± 4.433°, and 16.470° ± 8.362° in the incipient, immature, mature, and hypermature groups, respectively. For CCW, the mean ± SD was 0.351° ± 0.099°, 0.293° ± 0.121°, 0.315° ± 0.166°, and 0.352° ± 0.117° in the incipient, immature, mature, and hypermature groups, respectively (Table 1).

The mean ± SD value of age was 8.3 ± 3.1 years, 8.2 ± 3.5 years, 9.6 ± 2.8 years, and 10.2 ± 3.3 years in the incipient, immature, mature, and hypermature groups, respectively. The overall mean ± SD value of age in all groups was 8.9 ± 3.2 years (Fig. 3Fig. 3.
Box plot showing the age distribution in different stages of cataracts: incipient, immature, mature, and hypermature. The isolated dot was an outlier.).

In the analysis of demographic data from the patient, none of the variables showed significant differences between the eyes in the YP and SP groups (Table 2Table 2.Values of intraocular pressure and anterior segment parameters by age group
Age groupsNIOP (mmHg)ICA (°)CCW (mm)CCL (mm)CCA (mm2)
Young patients (≤8 years)2413.3 ± 3.313.890 ± 4.5940.312 ± 0.1112.028 ± 0.7040.474 ± 0.241
Old patients (≥9 years)2312.8 ± 3.715.402 ± 5.8770.346 ± 0.1332.349 ± 0.9050.514 ± 0.265
No statistical significance was observed. Mean ± SD values are shown. IOP, intraocular pressure; ICA, iridocorneal angle; CCW, width of the ciliary cleft; CCL, length of the ciliary cleft. CCA, area of the ciliary cleft.). We also performed the sex difference analysis, which did not show significant differences (Table 3Table 3.Values of intraocular pressure and anterior segment parameters by sex
SexNIOP (mmHg)ICA (°)CCW (mm)CCL (mm)CCA (mm2)
Male2313.1 ± 3.314.974 ± 6.0410.337 ± 0.1212.336 ± 0.7090.557 ± 0.271
Female2413.0 ± 3.714.300 ± 4.4910.320 ± 0.1252.039 ± 0.8980.434 ± 0.218
No statistical significance was observed. Mean ± SD values are shown. IOP, intraocular pressure; ICA, iridocorneal angle; CCW, width of the ciliary cleft; CCL, length of the ciliary cleft; CCA, area of the ciliary cleft.).

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: This study showed that the UBM parameters were associated with a narrower CCA and shorter CCL with cataract maturity. In contrast, the ICA, IOP, and CCW were not significantly correlated with cataract maturity. The UBM measurements were independent of age and sex.

In this study, a significant narrowing of the CCA was observed with increasing cataract maturity, while no significant association was observed between IOP and cataract maturity. Within the CC is the uveal trabecular meshwork, a spongiform cobweb-like tissue composed of irregular trabecular beams, and the spaces between these beams (Fontana’s spaces), which play an important role in the drainage of aqueous humor and IOP [21]. The CCA, as observed in the UBM, encompasses this area. It has previously been reported that there is a negative correlation between CCA and IOP, which differs from the findings of this study [1, 2, 10]. This study indicates that cataract progression may be associated with structural changes in the iridocorneal angle, although no significant differences in intraocular pressure (IOP) were observed across the stages. In humans, neovascular glaucoma occurs when pro-angiogenic factors such as vascular endothelial growth factor is activated in the presence of chronic uveitis, resulting in neovascularization of the iris. and iridocorneal angle, blocking the aqueous outflow pathway [23]. Increased angiogenic factors may contribute to the formation of fibrinous vascular material and the progression of CC narrowing, which may have led to an increase in IOP, although this increase was not significant in this study.

Cataracts can change the mechanical characteristics of the lenses. In humans, the position of the lens changes with age and cataracts [11]. It has been shown that mature cataract lenses thicken, although to a lesser extent than intumescent cataracts, while immature cataract lenses show thinning due to loss of lens protein in humans [28]. The change in the physical morphometry of the lens itself could explain the variation in CCA in this study. In contrast, another study compared the UBM parameters in dogs divided into two groups: a phacoemulsification surgery non-candidate group (including no-to-incipient cataracts) and a phacoemulsification surgery candidate group (including immature to mature cataracts). The study showed that CCA and CCW were significantly greater in the cataract surgery candidate group. The increased weight of the lens and relaxed ciliary muscle have been suggested to weaken the force that compresses the CC, leading to a larger CCA and CCW [13, 19]. The difference in results between the aforementioned study and this study can be attributed to differences in the subjects studied. The previous study did not include patients with hypermature cataracts, which could have reduced the volume of the lens due to resorption of the lens material, thus reducing the force on the CC. Furthermore, the accumulation of inflammatory substances in chronic LIU may have been more pronounced during the progression of hypermature cataracts in the subjects included in this study.

To date, few studies have evaluated changes in the CCL. In one of such studies, tafluprost, a prostaglandin analog known to reduce IOP, significantly restricted the pupil, decreased ICA, and increased CCW on UBM images [16]. However, it did not produce a significant change in the CCL. The disparity between their findings and those of this study is probably due to differences in study objectives. This study did not aim to assess the impact of miotic drugs on ICA structure. However, the narrowing of the CCA mentioned above could also explain the observed decrease in CCL as cataract maturity progressed (Table 1). Furthermore, the study mentioned above reported that although its results on CCA and CCL differed, its findings on CCW were similar to our study [13]. They confirmed that CCW increased as cataracts progressed from the immature to mature stages, which was part of their inclusion criteria and was consistent with the findings of this study [13].

Although not statistically significant, the IOP decreased from the incipient to the mature group and then increased again in the hypermature group. This observation was intriguing because cataract maturation was associated with decreased CCA and CCL, which are critical for regulating aqueous outflow. The reductions in CCA and CCL may have resulted from the accumulation of lens-induced uveitis-related inflammatory substances. This subclinical inflammation may explain the lower IOP observed during cataract progression [6,7,8, 22] to the mature stage. However, the increase in IOP in the hypermature group may indicate the point at which the effects of inflammation, which initially improve uveoscleral flow, begin to hinder aqueous outflow, leading to elevated IOP [18, 20, 26].

Differences in patient demographics, including age and sex, were not statistically significant for differences in IOP and UBM parameters in this study. In previous studies, 25 healthy male and female Beagles with no history of goniodysgenesis were evaluated using high-resolution ultrasound to measure angle opening distance (AOD) and angle recess area (ARA) [27]. The results showed that females had a significantly smaller AOD than males, which could explain their higher susceptibility to primary angle-closure glaucoma. Unlike a previous study that focused on AOD and ARA in relation to primary angle-closure glaucoma, which was located anteriorly in ICA, this study emphasized metrics related to posterior part of the CC. Additionally, while the previous study used healthy breed- and age-matched beagles (9–10 months old, 6–13 kg), the present study did not control for these factors, which may have introduced some variability.

Age was also found to be insignificant in relation to IOP and UBM parameters. However, the age distribution of the patients increased slightly as cataract maturity progressed, which should be considered in conjunction with previous discussions about IOP.

Kawata et al. emphasized that it may also be necessary to adjust the measurement values based on the size of each patient [12]. Focusing on the eyes of small breed dogs within the same ophthalmic examination setting, we assumed that the UBM parameter values for the ICA structures would require minimal adjustment. However, this study did not address potential breed-specific susceptibility. Furthermore, the findings of this study may only be universally applicable to some of the circumferences of the ICA because the examination was limited to the dorsolateral quadrant. It would be valuable to perform the same UBM parameter assessments in multiple quadrants of a single eye.

This study has a recruitment bias. The UBM imaging was performed primarily during preoperative evaluations, in cases of increased IOP compared to previous levels, or when there were concerns about possible development of glaucoma. The influence of iris configuration on the anterior segment structural parameters has been well documented in several studies [5, 16]. Controlling these effects requires optimization of the experimental conditions. However, this is a retrospective study. It was also limited to UBM data obtained without the administration of topical mydriatics. In addition, the relatively small sample size may have limited the identification of significant age- and sex-related effects. This study primarily included small-breed dogs predisposed to cataracts and glaucoma, such as Poodles, Bichon Frises, and Shih Tzus. Although we carefully eliminated eyes where primary angle-closure glaucoma (PACG) appeared to have affected the opposite eye based on medical records, the potential influence of breed-related glaucoma predisposition cannot be entirely excluded. Additionally, breed-specific statistical analysis was not conducted due to the sample size, which may limit the generalizability of findings to other breeds or populations.

Unlike previous studies that examined UBM parameters and IOP after changes in iris morphology, such as miosis or mydriasis and after phacoemulsification surgery, this study aimed to minimize the influence of the iris. It specifically identifies changes in UBM images that are likely caused by cataract maturation. These findings suggest that these changes are related to cataract progression or age-related factors. Future research should explore histological changes in the posterior part of the CC of normotensive cataract eyes. In conclusion, using UBM to evaluate the posterior part of the CC in cataractous eyes may be beneficial to assess glaucoma risk and consider prophylactic treatment when cataracts should be managed without surgery and before performing phacoemulsification surgery for mature to hypermature cataracts.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ack.p: This study was supported by the Research Institute for Veterinary Science (RIVS), College of Veterinary Medicine, Seoul National University. In addition, this research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (2021R1I1A1A01058695).
